feed.title: Re: March 23rd 2021 Taproot Activation Meeting Notes

entry.title: Re: March 23rd 2021 Taproot Activation Meeting Notes
entry.summary:
The correspondence raises concerns and critiques regarding the decision-making process in the development of blockchain technology, specifically addressing the issue of using Median Time Past (MTP) versus block height for soft forks in the Bitcoin protocol. The initial point of contention is the criticism of dismissing MTP-based Soft Forks (ST) as "weird," which the author argues lacks technical merit. Highlighting the absence of a robust argument against MTP-based ST, the author references a technical justification for employing MTP with a minimum active height, as detailed in a [GitHub comment](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/21377issuecomment-792425221). This reference is presented to counter the claim that choosing MTP is an odd or unjustified decision, suggesting instead that without additional adjustments, MTP provides a viable mechanism for implementing changes.
Furthermore, the letter delves into the implications of prioritizing block height over MTP for soft forks, suggesting that such a preference inadvertently supports specific marketing strategies of certain projects, thereby introducing biases into the technical review process. This stance is critiqued for potentially placing undue burden on contributors by favoring one implementation method over another based on external factors unrelated to technical efficacy or consensus stability.
Additionally, the author disputes the rationale behind opposing MTP-based ST by pointing out that it is, in fact, compatible with a proposed implementation strategy that includes both block height (BIP8 LOT=true) and a minimum active height requirement. This compatibility undermines arguments against MTP-based ST, indicating that objections may stem from misconceptions or a lack of understanding regarding its technical feasibility and potential benefits within the broader context of blockchain governance and protocol upgrades.
In essence, the letter calls for a more nuanced and technically grounded discussion on the merits of different approaches to soft forks in the Bitcoin network, challenging the dismissal of MTP-based strategies without substantial technical critique. It emphasizes the need for open-minded consideration of all viable options to ensure that decisions reflect the best interests of the network and its participants, rather than being swayed by external pressures or unfounded preferences.
